method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name Nishinoshima literally means "western island".[2] Before receiving its current Japanese name, the island was first recorded by the Spanish ship Rosario in 1702 and was known as Rosario Island.[3]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active volcanic island in the Ogasawara Islands, located on the Izu–Bonin arc where volcanism is driven by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] It lies about 1,000 km south of Tokyo and about 130 km west of Chichijima.[4]

Geologically, the island is the emergent summit of a large submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter.[1] The volcanic edifice includes the main peak exposed above sea level as well as two submarine peaks to the west and south, and a small cone on the northeast flank that may be a satellite cone.[4] Nishinoshima has been described as an andesitic volcanic island, although its recent eruptive products show a wider compositional range.[1][3]

The island present before the modern eruptive phase was about 650 m long and 200 m wide, with an area of roughly 77,000 m2 and a maximum elevation of 25 m.[1] Several reefs occur off its northeastern side.[1] Submarine stratovolcanoes with basal diameters of 20–30 km and heights of 2,200–2,800 m characterize the surrounding region north of Nishinoshima, placing it within a broader volcanic province of large arc volcanoes.[4]

The first recorded historical eruption at Nishinoshima began in 1973 on the sea floor east of the island’s southern tip.[1] A new island was discovered in July 1973, and by September it had grown to about 150 m in diameter and 40 m in height, accompanied by volcanic blocks and water columns generated by phreatomagmatic activity.[1] The eruption developed through multiple craters, with lava flows and successive small islets that were eventually connected above sea level in October 1973.[1] Erosion later removed much of the individual eruptive edifices, but redistributed deposits joined the old and new islands, and by 1982 sediment movement had closed the mouth of the bay between them.[1]

The 1973–1974 products had SiO2 contents ranging from 51.8 to 60.1 wt%, indicating compositions from basaltic andesite to andesite.[1] More recent petrological work on the 2020 eruption showed that Nishinoshima erupted basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite magmas, while the andesites resemble those erupted from the island since 1973.[3] These studies suggest that Nishinoshima’s andesite primary magmas originate directly from the mantle through shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite, and that basaltic magmas have probably been generated throughout the volcano’s history, although their eruptive center shifted to the island summit in 2020.[3]

Since 2013, Nishinoshima has undergone a prolonged eruptive sequence divided into four episodes.[3] The 2013–2015 eruption and the 2017 eruption were dominated by lava effusion, with average effusion rates estimated at 2.0 × 10^5 m3/day and 1.5 × 10^5 m3/day respectively.[3] During this activity, a new island formed in November 2013 and soon merged with the pre-existing island, greatly enlarging Nishinoshima.[2] By 2020, eruptive behavior had changed markedly: Episode 4 began with Strombolian explosions and lava effusion, progressed to lava fountains, and ended with ash emissions interpreted as likely phreatomagmatic eruptions, a style not observed in the island’s earlier recent activity.[4]

Because the volcano repeatedly resurfaces the island with new lava and tephra, its geology strongly controls the development of soils, landforms, and biota.[5] The major 2013 eruption, for example, destroyed nearly all vegetation on the island, demonstrating how recurrent volcanism periodically resets the island surface.[5]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima has been described as a natural laboratory for primary succession because repeated eruptions repeatedly remove terrestrial biota and create newly available habitat for recolonization.[2][3][4] The island’s ecology is therefore characterized less by stable mature communities than by recurrent cycles of local extinction, immigration, and early-stage ecosystem development.[2][3]

Within the broader Ogasawara archipelago, Nishinoshima forms part of an oceanic island biota noted for high endemism and independent evolution from mainland Asia, although ecological conditions differ markedly among islands.[3][5] More generally, the Ogasawara Islands are recognized for adaptive radiation, speciation, and the preservation of distinctive oceanic-island ecosystems, which gives Nishinoshima wider biogeographical importance despite its small size and instability.[1][5]

Botanical observations have documented plant invasion and colonization on the island after eruptive episodes, and Nishinoshima has been specifically cited as a site for studying the establishment of vegetation on a new volcanic island.[4] Ongoing monitoring has also focused on rates of colonization and ecosystem development on the expanding island, including adjacent marine habitats such as coral reefs.[3]

The island’s biota is highly vulnerable to renewed volcanism, which can almost completely eliminate existing vegetation.[2] A 2019 eruption was reported to have caused the local extinction of the native population of purslane, Portulaca oleracea, on Nishinoshima, after some plants had been removed for ex situ conservation by researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University.[2] This sensitivity to disturbance has made Nishinoshima an important case study for resilience and recolonization in isolated Pacific island ecosystems.[3][5]
